Summary 
20% 
of the 
acetylcholine 
receptors 
in cultured 
chick 
skeletal 
muscle 
remain 
unbound 
following 
long-term 
growth 
of muscle 
in medium 
containing 
a potent, 
essentially 
irreversible 
receptor-block- 
ing agent, 
cu-bungarotoxin. 
About 
half the recep- 
tors 
which 
are 
unavailable 
for interaction 
with 
extracellular 
cw-bungarotoxin 
are 
newly 
synthe- 
sized 
molecules 
which 
presumably 
are 
being 
processed 
and 
transported 
to the 
plasma 
mem- 
brane. 
When 
the muscle 
cultures 
are switched 
to 
a medium 
containing 
2H, ‘YZ, 15N-amino 
acids, 
these 
receptors 
are rapidly 
labeled, 
the fraction 
of labeled 
molecules 
beginning 
to plateau 
at 3 hr. 
Few 
labeled 
receptors 
appear 
in 
the 
plasma 
membrane 
during 
the first 3 hr of labeling 
with 
‘H, 
‘YZ, 15N-amino 
acids. 
After 
3.5 hr of labeling, 
vir- 
tually 
all the receptors 
being 
incorporated 
into the 
plasma 
membrane 
are labeled 
receptors. 
The 
ki- 
netics 
of labeling 
of the 
“pool” 
and 
“surface” 
receptors 
with 
‘H, 13C, 15N-amino 
acids 
confirm 
the “precursor-product” 
type relationship 
of pool 
and surface 
acetylcholine 
receptors. 
In this study, 
receptors 
synthesized 
in medium 
containing 
‘H, 13C, 15N-amino 
acids were 
resolved 
from ‘H, ‘X, 
14N-receptors 
by velocity 
sedimenta- 
tion in sucrose-deuterium 
oxide 
and sucrose-Hz0 
gradients, 
and 
their 
densities 
were 
estimated 
from sedimentation 
rates 
in shallow 
gradients 
of 
various 
average 
density. 
Estimated 
densities 
were 
1.32 
g/cm3 
for ‘H, 
lzC, 
“N-receptors 
and 
1.41 g/cm3 
for ‘H, 13C, 15N-receptors. 
This density 
difference 
corresponds 
to 80% substitution 
of nor- 
mal aminoacyl 
residues 
by *H, 13C, ‘5N-residues 
in the denser 
receptor. 
Introduction 
In previous 
reports, 
the kinetics 
of turnover 
of’ace- 
tylcholine 
receptors 
in membranes 
of developing 
chick 
muscle 
fibers 
have been 
described 
and the 
results 
of some 
inhibitor 
studies 
have 
been 
pre- 
sented 
(Hartzell 
and Fambrough, 
1973; 
Devreotes 
and Fambrough, 
1975, 
1976a, 
l976b). 
This 
report 
focuses 
on one 
aspect 
of receptor 
metabolism- 
intracellular 
processing 
of newly 
synthesized 
re- 
ceptors. 
A model 
has been presented 
which 
describes 
the 
biosynthesis 
and degradation 
of acetylcholine 
re- 
ceptors 
(Devreotes 
and 
Fambrough, 
1976b). 
This 
model 
is based 
upon 
studies 
which 
suggest 
that 
receptors 
are synthesized 
de novo in the myotubes 
and undergo 
a 3 hr transit 
before 
exposure 
on the 
myotube 
surface. 
This intracellular 
transport 
proc- 
ess is independent 
of protein 
synthesis, 
but is en- 
ergy-dependent 
and temperature-dependent. 
Pur- 
omycin 
and cycloheximide 
have little effect on the 
appearance 
of new receptors 
at the surface 
for 2-3 
hr, but after this initial 
lag, become 
effective 
inhibi- 
tors. 
2; 4-dinitrophenol 
(DNP) 
and 
carbonyl-M- 
chlorophenyl 
hydrazone 
(FCCP) 
block 
incorpora- 
tion of new receptors 
into the surface 
membrane 
at 
37°C from 
the outset. 
Similarly, 
incorporation 
of 
new 
receptors 
cannot 
proceed 
at low 
tempera- 
tures. 
The observation 
that 
new 
receptors 
continue 
to 
appear 
at the surface 
for 2-3 hr in the absence 
of 
protein 
synthesis 
suggested 
that a pool 
of presyn- 
thesized 
receptors 
exists. 
The 
“pool 
receptors” 
were 
identified 
in nonionic 
detergent 
extracts 
of 
myogenic 
cell cultures 
(Devreotes 
and Fambrough, 
1975). All the soluble 
a-bungarotoxin 
binding 
sites 
considered 
to represent 
pool 
receptors 
were 
d- 
tubocurarine-competed 
and 
had a sedimentation 
coefficient 
of about 
10s. The 
number 
of these 
ct- 
bungarotoxin 
binding 
sites, 
or 
receptors, 
de- 
creased 
rapidly 
when 
muscle 
cultures 
were 
treated 
with 
puromycin. 
The 
kinetics 
of decrease 
of pool 
receptors 
approximately 
matched 
the kinetics 
of 
appearance 
of new 
surface 
receptors 
in the pres- 
ence 
of puromycin-that 
is, the pool 
was 
signifi- 
cantly depleted 
at the time new receptors 
ceased 
to 
appear 
at the surface.

In this report,

This technique

the
